1: The historical superscription — 'when he fled from Absalom his son' — grounds the Psalm in 2 Sam 15-17, the most devastating political crisis of David's reign. Absalom spent four years systematically undermining confidence in David's justice by intercepting litigants at the city gate and declaring 'If only I were appointed judge, I would give you justice' (2 Sam 15:2-4). He then launched a carefully organized coup from Hebron, David's first capital, and David fled Jerusalem on foot, barefoot and with head covered (the mourning posture), crossing the Kidron Valley eastward in a reversal of the triumphal processions that had brought the ark to Zion.
2: 'There is no salvation for him in God (elohim)' — in the ancient Near East, a king's defeat or exile was interpreted as his deity having withdrawn favor. The Akkadian concept of melammu (the divine aura of kingship) departing from a ruler was used to explain military defeat. The Bathsheba/Uriah incident and Nathan's subsequent curse (2 Sam 12:10-12: 'The sword will never depart from your house') gave David's enemies theological ammunition: they could point to David's own prophetic condemnation as proof that YHWH had abandoned him. The taunt was historically grounded in David's documented moral failure.
3: 'You, O LORD, are a shield around me, my glory, and the one who lifts my head' — in the honor-shame world of ancient Israel, a man's social standing was manifested in his head: the lifted head was the posture of dignity (Gen 40:13: 'Pharaoh will lift up your head' = restore your honor); the bowed or downcast head was the posture of shame and defeat (Ps 44:25: 'our soul is bowed down to the dust'). YHWH as 'the one who lifts my head' is the patron who restores the client's honor in the face of rivals. Against the taunts of v2, YHWH himself is David's reputation-defender.
4: 'I cried out to the LORD, and he answered me from his holy hill' — 2 Sam 15:30-32 locates David on the top of the Mount of Olives when he learns of Ahithophel's defection. He immediately prayed (2 Sam 15:31: 'Turn Ahithophel's counsel into foolishness, O LORD'). YHWH's answer 'from his holy hill' (Zion, where the ark and the altar remained) represents the paradox: the king is in exile from the city, but the covenant communication network remains intact. The physical distance between David and the temple does not sever the covenant access to YHWH's presence.
5: 'I lay down and slept; I awoke again, for the LORD sustained me' — the ability to sleep is a confession of trust. 2 Sam 17:1-3 reports Ahithophel's urgent counsel: 'Let me pursue David tonight while he is weary and weak — I will come upon him while he is sleeping and terrify all the people who are with him.' That David slept in this context — whether as historical record or spiritual type — demonstrates that covenant trust creates security that military alertness cannot manufacture. The companion Psalm 4:8 ('in peace I will lie down and sleep, for you alone, O LORD, make me dwell in safety') is the explicit evening counterpart to this morning psalm.
6: 'I will not fear tens of thousands who have surrounded me on every side' — the covenant confidence that numerical disadvantage does not determine outcome echoes the warfare theology of the entire OT: five will chase a hundred and a hundred will chase ten thousand (Lev 26:8); 'nothing can hinder the LORD from saving by many or by few' (1 Sam 14:6, Jonathan's declaration before his single-handed attack on the Philistine garrison). Gideon's 300 versus Midian and the army of Aram struck blind before Elisha are the narrative instantiations of the same principle. Israel's covenant warfare theology was structurally opposed to the ANE calculus of victory through superior numbers.
7: 'Rise up, O LORD! Save me, O my God!' — this is the battle cry of Num 10:35: 'Whenever the ark set out, Moses said, Rise up, O LORD! Let your enemies be scattered; let those who hate you flee before you.' David invokes the ark-warfare tradition — the tradition that YHWH's presence (not military strength) was the decisive factor — even though he is physically separated from the ark. The ark is on Zion; David is on the Mount of Olives. The invocation affirms that covenant access to divine power is not geographically conditional on proximity to the cultic apparatus.
8: 'Salvation belongs to the LORD; your blessing be upon your people' — the foundational principle of the Psalter's lament tradition: the outcome of human crisis is in the divine patron's hands, not the petitioner's or his military commanders'. The Psalm that began with David's personal crisis ends with intercession for the whole covenant community: the king's prayer extends beyond personal deliverance to the welfare of Israel. This movement — from individual lament to communal blessing — is characteristic of the royal psalms, where the king's covenant standing and the nation's welfare are interconnected.
